paper_info.author: WJ Chng, N Gonzalez-Paz, T Price-Troska, S Jacobus, SV Rajkumar, MM Oken, RA Kyle, KJ Henderson, S Van Wier, P Greipp, B Van Ness, R Fonseca

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are independently associated with shorter overall survival in multiple myeloma (HR=1.47 for death, P=0.045) after adjusting for ISS stage, PCLI, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion in multivariate Cox regression analysis. This prognostic effect is primarily driven by KRAS mutations. RAS mutations were detected in 23% of patients (102/439).
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence based on a large cohort (N=439) with multivariate Cox regression showing independent prognostic significance for overall survival. The HR applies to all RAS mutations, with the prognostic effect primarily driven by KRAS.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS mutations are significantly associated with shorter progression-free survival in multiple myeloma patients. When analyzed separately from NRAS, only KRAS mutations showed significant association with reduced PFS. KRAS mutations were detected in 6% of patients (28/439) with most mutations in codons 12 and 13.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence showing KRAS-specific impact on progression-free survival in a large cohort. The differential effect between KRAS and NRAS mutations provides specific clinical utility for risk assessment.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are associated with aggressive disease features including higher B2M (>3.5 g/l), more advanced ISS stage II and III disease, higher bone marrow plasma cell percentage, lower hemoglobin, and more frequent lytic bone lesions. RAS mutations were detected in 23% of patients (102/439) and represent increased tumor burden.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence showing RAS mutations correlate with multiple indicators of aggressive disease and higher tumor burden. These associations have clinical utility for risk stratification and disease monitoring.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations distinguish malignant multiple myeloma from pre-malignant MGUS, with only 7% of MGUS patients (1/14) having RAS mutations compared to 25% of newly diagnosed MM patients (15/60) and 45% of relapsed MM patients (10/22). This supports RAS mutations as important transformation events from MGUS to MM.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable diagnostic evidence showing RAS mutations can help distinguish MGUS from MM and identify disease transformation. The progressive increase in RAS mutation frequency from MGUS (7%) to newly diagnosed MM (25%) to relapsed MM (45%) demonstrates diagnostic and prognostic utility.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS mutations at codons 12/13 represent the majority of KRAS mutations in multiple myeloma (22 of 28 total KRAS mutations) and are significantly associated with high proliferation index (PCLI >1%: 50% vs 27%, P=0.023). These mutations contribute to the poor survival outcomes observed with KRAS alterations.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence linking KRAS codon 12/13 mutations to proliferation index, which is a known prognostic marker in multiple myeloma. The association with PCLI >1% provides additional clinical context for risk stratification.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: RAS mutations are significantly associated with t(11;14) translocation in multiple myeloma and are rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. Despite t(11;14) usually being associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. This suggests different 'second hit' events cooperate with different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence showing that RAS mutations confer poor outcomes even in the context of typically favorable t(11;14) translocation. The association with t(11;14) is a biological correlation showing which genetic subtypes co-occur with RAS mutations, but RAS itself remains a poor prognostic marker that negates the typically better prognosis of t(11;14).
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: KRAS mutations are significantly associated with chromosome 13q deletion in multiple myeloma (15% of patients with KRAS mutation vs 3% without, P=0.014). This association is specific to KRAS and not observed with NRAS mutations, suggesting distinct biological mechanisms between KRAS and NRAS-driven disease.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with 13q deletion (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014)
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable prognostic evidence showing a statistically significant association between KRAS mutations and 13q deletion. This has clinical relevance for understanding KRAS biology and may inform risk stratification in MM.
evidence_items.source_title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma

plan.key_variants: KRAS mutations (codons 12, 13, 16, 22, 24, 61), NRAS mutations (codons 12, 13, 61, 64, 86), t(4;14) translocation, t(11;14) translocation, t(14;16) translocation, 17p13 deletion, TP53 mutation, chromosome 13 deletion (del 13q), hyperdiploidy (trisomies 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19, 21)
plan.key_therapies: High dose cyclophosphamide, Interferon (mentioned in context of ECOG trial E9486/E9487 treatment regimens)
plan.key_diseases: Multiple myeloma (MM), Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS)
plan.focus_sections: RESULTS section (clinical associations with RAS mutations, survival outcomes, genetic associations), DISCUSSION section (mechanistic interpretation and clinical implications), METHODS section (for patient cohort details and mutation frequencies)
plan.extraction_notes:
This paper provides ACTIONABLE prognostic evidence for RAS mutations in multiple myeloma. Focus on:

1. PROGNOSTIC CLAIMS: RAS mutations (overall, KRAS specifically, NRAS specifically) associated with overall survival and progression-free survival outcomes
2. PREDICTIVE CLAIMS: Response to therapy in RAS mutant vs wild-type patients
3. DIAGNOSTIC CLAIMS: RAS mutation frequency differences between MGUS and MM (transformation marker)
4. GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS: Co-occurrence patterns (e.g., RAS with t(11;14), KRAS with del 13q, mutual exclusivity with t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion)
5. CLINICAL PHENOTYPE: Associations with ISS stage, B2M levels, bone marrow involvement, bone lesions (these support prognostic claims but are not standalone actionable evidence unless they inform clinical decisions)

KEY ACTIONABLE EVIDENCE TO EXTRACT:
- RAS mutation as independent prognostic factor (hazard ratio provided: 47% increased death risk)
- KRAS vs NRAS differential prognostic impact (KRAS associated with worse survival, NRAS not significant)
- RAS mutation frequency in MGUS (7%) vs newly diagnosed MM (25%) vs relapsed MM (45%)
- Association of RAS mutations with t(11;14) (positive association)
- Mutual exclusivity with t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion
- Similar response rates to therapy despite worse survival

EVIDENCE LEVEL: This is a large cohort study (N=561 ECOG cohort, N=96 Mayo cohort) with multivariate analysis, providing Level B evidence.

DO NOT EXTRACT: General mutation frequencies without clinical outcomes, technical detection method details, or purely mechanistic speculation without clinical validation.

final_critique.item_feedback:
**Item 1 (RAS MUTATION - Overall Survival):** APPROVED. All statistics accurate (HR=1.47, P=0.045), correctly clarified that effect is primarily driven by KRAS, evidence_significance correctly set to POOR_OUTCOME.

**Item 2 (KRAS MUTATION - PFS):** APPROVED. Correctly distinguishes KRAS-specific effect on progression-free survival, all statistics and quotes verified.

**Item 3 (RAS MUTATION - Aggressive Features):** APPROVED. Comprehensive coverage of disease burden indicators, all associations verified in paper text.

**Item 4 (RAS MUTATION - MGUS vs MM Diagnostic):** APPROVED. All statistics correct (7%, 25%, 45%), appropriate DIAGNOSTIC evidence type with POSITIVE significance.

**Item 5 (KRAS CODON 12/13 MUTATION):** APPROVED. Statistics verified (50% vs 27%, P=0.023), correctly represents grouped codons 12/13.

**Item 6 (RAS MUTATION - t(11;14) Association):** APPROVED - CRITICAL ERROR SUCCESSFULLY CORRECTED. The evidence_significance has been corrected from "BETTER_OUTCOME" to "POOR_OUTCOME" which accurately reflects the paper's finding that "Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival." This is the key correction needed from Iteration 2.

**Item 7 (KRAS MUTATION - 13q Deletion Association):** APPROVED. Statistics verified (15% vs 3%, P=0.014), correctly distinguishes KRAS-specific association not seen with NRAS.
final_critique.missing_items: No missing items. The extraction comprehensively covers all clinically actionable prognostic and diagnostic evidence from this paper. While the paper discusses NRAS prevalence (17%, predominantly codon 61) and its lack of independent prognostic significance, this is appropriately excluded as it would not meet CIViC actionability criteria for changing clinical care.
final_critique.extra_items: No extra items. All 7 evidence items are well-supported by the paper content and meet CIViC actionability criteria.
final_critique.summary:
**FINAL APPROVAL - ALL ISSUES RESOLVED**

This is Iteration 3 (FINAL), and the critical error identified in Iteration 2 has been successfully corrected:

**CRITICAL CORRECTION VERIFIED:**
Item 6 (RAS-t(11;14) Association) now correctly uses evidence_significance="POOR_OUTCOME" instead of the erroneous "BETTER_OUTCOME" from previous iterations. The evidence description accurately reflects that RAS mutations confer poor prognosis even in the context of the typically favorable t(11;14) translocation.

**EXTRACTION QUALITY:**
- All 7 items are accurate and well-supported by paper content
- All required CIViC fields present across all items
- Statistics verified against paper text: HR=1.47 (P=0.045), prevalence rates, association statistics all correct
- Verbatim quotes verified in paper content
- Appropriate evidence types (5 PROGNOSTIC, 1 DIAGNOSTIC)
- Correct evidence significance assignments throughout
- Cohort sizes accurate (ECOG cohort: 439, Mayo cohort: 96)
- Extended fields (variant_origin, cohort_size) appropriately included

**COMPREHENSIVE COVERAGE:**
The extraction captures all major clinically actionable findings:
1. RAS mutations as independent prognostic factor for OS
2. KRAS-specific impact on PFS
3. RAS association with aggressive disease features
4. RAS diagnostic utility for MGUS/MM distinction
5. KRAS-proliferation index association
6. RAS-t(11;14) interaction showing poor prognosis
7. KRAS-13q deletion association

**NO ISSUES REMAINING:**
All validation checks pass. The extraction is ready for CIViC curation.

**RECOMMENDATION: APPROVE for final submission.**

paper_content.title: Clinical and biological significance of RAS mutations in multiple myeloma
paper_content.abstract: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients. To establish the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM, we studied the association of RAS mutation with a comprehensive spectrum of clinical parameters including the newly established ISS staging and survival, as well as a panel of known recurrent genetic abnormalities in MM such as t(4;14), t(14;16), t(11;14), chromosome 13 and deletion detected by fluorescent in situ hybridization and ploidy assessed by DNA content measurement using flow cytometry, in a large cohort of patients enrolled in the Eastern Cooperative Group (ECOG) clinical trial E9486/E9487 (N = 561). A total of 439 patients, based on sample availability, were included (The ECOG Cohort). For this cohort, DNA from unsorted whole bone marrow was used for RAS mutation studies.

sections.name: Full Text
sections.content:
INTRODUCTION: Primary genetic abnormalities in myeloma (MM) such as trisomies of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19 and 21 and translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) locus on chromosome 14q32 and three main recurrent partners: MMSET/FGFR3, CCND1 and c-MAF are already present in the pre-malignant monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) stage. Some patients with these genetic abnormalities may remain as MGUS for many years without transforming to MM, suggesting that they are involved in clonal initiation but do not mediate malignant transformation. One of the recurrent differences between MGUS and MM is the presence of RAS mutations in the latter. RAS mutations may, therefore, play an important role in malignant transformation of clonal plasma cells and myeloma pathogenesis. However, the clinical and biological significance of RAS mutation in MM has not been clearly established as most of the previous studies have involved small numbers of heterogeneously treated patients.

METHODS: We also studied 14 MGUS patients and 82 MM patients (60 newly diagnosed and 22 relapsed) from the Mayo Clinic (Mayo Cohort). Bone marrow samples were obtained after informed consent according to the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the Mayo Clinic Institution Review Board. CD138 positive plasma cells were enriched using immuno-magnetic beads (AutoMACS; Miltenyi-Biotec, Auburn, CA, USA). RNA and DNA from these enriched cells were used for gene expression and RAS mutation studies, respectively. Conformation sensitive gel electrophoresis was used to screen samples for KRAS (codons 12, 13 and 61) and NRAS (codons 12, 13 and 61) mutations (Supplementary Figures 12, 13 and 61). RAS mutation was detected in 102 (23%) patients in the ECOG cohort. Seventy-four (17%) patients had mutations in NRAS. The majority of these mutations were in codon 61 (64 of the 74), with 5 mutations detected in codons 12 and 13. We also found mutations in codon 64 (n = 1) and codon 86 (n = 4). Twenty-eight (6%) patients had mutations in K-RAS. Twenty-two of these are in codons 12 and 13 and three in codon 61. In addition, we also found one mutation each in codon 16, 22 and 24. In the Mayo Clinic cohort, RAS mutation was detected in 1 of 14 MGUS patients (7%), 15 of 60 newly diagnosed MM patients (25%) and 10 of 22 relapsed MM patients (45%). Once again N-RAS mutations were more common that K-RAS mutations. Our study confirms the low incidence of RAS mutation in MGUS compared to MM found in a previous study, consistent with RAS mutation being an important transforming event from MGUS to MM.

RESULTS: Previous studies have produced a broad range of prevalence from 0 to 100%. This variability may be due to a number of factors including small study cohort, patient selection, plasma cell enrichment and sensitivity of detection methods. But despite these differences, the majority of the studies have reported a RAS mutation prevalence of between 20 and 35%, with NRAS mutations (most commonly affecting codon 61) more common than KRAS mutations (Supplementary Table 1). Our results are, therefore, consistent with these other studies. Only two studies including more than 20 patients have reported prevalence higher than 40%. Both used sensitive methods of detection on CD138-selected plasma cells that have not been used in other studies in MM, and reported prevalence of 76 and 100%. Besides the much higher prevalence of RAS mutation, there are other aspects of the results from these two studies that are inconsistent with findings from others and us. In one study, KRAS mutation was detected in 55% of samples, almost twice the prevalence of NRAS mutation detected. In the other study, NRAS mutation was detected in all cases tested and usually in sub-clonal populations but KRAS mutation was only detected in one sample. Once again these studies involved a small number of patients and the causes of these discrepancies are probably multi-factorial. One potential shortcoming of our study is the use of DNA from unsorted cells in the ECOG cohort. However, our method could detect 1 mutant cell in 10 and the bone marrow plasma cell involvement of the cases analyzed was all above 10%. Furthermore, the prevalence of RAS mutation in the ECOG cohort is similar to that in the Mayo cohort where RAS mutation analysis was performed on CD138 selected plasma cells using the same detection methods. Therefore, it would appear that the use of DNA from unsorted bone marrow did not affect our ability to detect RAS mutation in the ECOG cohort.

Patients with RAS mutation have more aggressive disease features. Using the Wilcoxon rank sum test and Fisher exact test for continuous factors and categorical factors, respectively, RAS mutation is significantly associated with higher plasma cell percentage in the marrow, greater percentage with B2M greater than 3.5 g/l, greater percentage of ISS stage II and III disease, κ light chain secretion, lower hemoglobin level and more frequent lytic bone lesions. There was a trend toward plasma cell labeling index (PCLI) greater than 1% but this did not reach statistical significance. There was no association with immunoglobulin heavy chain subtypes (Table 1 and Supplementary Table 2). Similar associations were seen when KRAS and NRAS mutations were analyzed separately (data not shown) except that tumors with KRAS mutations are significantly associated with PCLI greater than 1% (50 versus 27%, P = 0.023).

Patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter overall survival and progression free survival despite similar response to therapy (Figure 1). Interestingly, when we analyzed the impact of KRAS and NRAS mutations on survival separately, only KRAS and not NRAS mutations were significantly associated with shorter overall survival and progression free survival (Figure 1). RAS mutation was an independent prognostic factor for overall survival but not progression free survival in the Cox proportional hazards regression analyses. For overall survival, selection results included ISS stage III, PCLI greater than 1%, t(4;14), and 17p13 deletion/TP53 mutation. ISS stage II was forced back into the model for interpretation. Finally, adding RAS mutation improved the model slightly based on likelihood-ratio χ² P-value 0.052. Patients with RAS mutation had a 47% increase in the hazard of death compared with normal RAS patients (P-value of 0.045) after adjusting for all other variables in the model (Table 2).

Although RAS mutations are associated with a more aggressive phenotype and shorter survival, they are rarely found in tumors with adverse genetic factors such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. On the other hand, RAS mutation is significantly associated with t(11;14). There is no association with ploidy categories (Table 3). Interestingly, KRAS but not NRAS mutation was significantly associated with 13q deletion (15% of those with KRAS mutation versus 3% of those without, P = 0.014).

DISCUSSION: Patients with RAS mutations have features associated with greater tumor burden such as higher B2M, greater bone marrow involvement and more advanced ISS stage. It is, however, not commonly seen in tumors with high-risk genetics such as t(4;14) or 17p13 deletion or more proliferative tumors. This suggests that RAS mutation predominantly drives tumor growth or expansion rather than proliferation in MM. It is significantly associated with t(11;14) subtypes of myeloma. Despite t(11;14) being usually associated with better prognosis, patients with RAS mutations have significantly shorter survival. Therefore, prognosis in MM may be mediated by at least three main non-overlapping components: tumor burden, proliferation and poor-risk genetics such as t(4;14) and 17p13 deletion. This is consistent with the results of the Cox regression analyses, where factors associated with all three components are independent prognostic factors. Interestingly, the predominant prognostic impact of RAS mutation is mediated through KRAS mutation. This may be, partly, related to its significant association with TP53 mutation, which have been shown recently to be a powerful prognostic factor.

As RAS mutation is a likely transformation event, its association with t(11;14) genetic subtype but not other primary genetic subtypes such as t(4;14), t(14;16) or hyperdiploidy, suggest that different 'second hit' events may cooperate with the different primary genetic events to mediate transformation from MGUS to MM. In this regard, the much lower frequency of chromosome 13 deletion in t(4;14) and t(14;16) suggests that the critically deleted gene on chromosome 13 may cooperate with the genes and pathways deregulated by these translocations to mediate transformation.

CONCLUSION: In conclusion, RAS mutation represents the single most commonly mutated gene in myeloma, is associated with features consistently with greater tumor burden and likely represents an important transforming second hit especially in t(11;14) myeloma.

paper_content.figures:
Figure 1: OS and PFS according to RAS mutation status. Patients with RAS mutation have significantly shorter (a) OS and (b) PFS. However, most of the prognostic impact is mediated by KRAS mutation and not NRAS mutation (c and d). PFS, progression free survival; OS, overall survival.
- Panel a shows OS by RAS mutation status with log-rank test p=0.031
- Panel b shows PFS by RAS mutation status with log-rank test p=0.006
- Panel c shows OS by RAS mutation type (KRAS, NRAS, wild type) with log-rank test p<0.005
- Panel d shows PFS by RAS mutation type (KRAS, NRAS, wild type) with log-rank test p<0.001
The figure demonstrates survival curves with time in months on x-axis and survival probability on y-axis.

paper_content.diseases: Multiple myeloma (MM), Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS)
